coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of Gd3+ doped carbon quantum dots: Photoluminescence materials with magnetic resonance response as magnetic resonance/fluorescence bimodal probes

Liu, C.L.
Qin, Y.
Guo, L.P.
Liu, L.H.

Photoluminescence
Magnetic resonance
Bimodal probes
Doping

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence quantum yield and the Gd3+ ions content of Gd3+-CQDs obtained at different temperature for 8 h (a), and at 220 °C for different reaction time (b).

(a) TEM image of Gd3+-CQDs nanoclusters which were aggregate of tiny Gd3+-CQDs particles. (b) A higher-magnification TEM image of a Gd3+-CQDs nanoclusters (the white round frame in (c) and (d) show the same Gd3+-CQDs nanoclusters). (c) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of Gd3+-CQDs (top inset: SAED pattern in TEM mode; lower inset: high resolution TEM image). (d) size distribution of Gd3+-CQDs obtained from the TEM image.
Wide scan XPS spectra of Gd3+-CQDs (a); high resolution spectra of Gd3d (b) and Gd4d (c) in Gd3+-CQDs prepared at different temperature; IR spectra of GdPM and Gd3+-CQDs (d).

(a) Absorption (ABS) and fluorescence (FLSC, 365 nm excitation) spectra of the Gd3+-CQDs in an aqueous solution; (b) fluorescence emission spectra of Gd3+-CQDs obtained with different excitation wavelengths (inset: the normalized PL spectra).

(a) T
1 -weighted MR images (1: H2O; 2: GdPM; 3: Magnevist; 4: as-prepared Gd3+-CQDs, [Gd3+] = 0.01 mM]) and (b) Linear relationship between T
1 and Gd3+ concentration for as-prepared Gd3+-CQDs.
Viability of HeLa cells incubated with various concentrations of Gd3+-CQDs for 24 h.
The pH value of GdPM solution (pH1), and that after hydrothermal treated under different hydrothermal conditions (pH2).

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of Gd3+ doped carbon quantum dots: Photoluminescence materials with magnetic resonance response as magnetic resonance/fluorescence bimodal probes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang, 621010, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Southwest University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: First Affiliated Hospital of Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, 710061, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: First Affiliated Hospital of Xi'an Jiaotong University

ce:section.ce:para: Modern clinical cancer treatments require precise positional information. Where is the tumor located? How large is it? Is it confined, or has it spread [1]? However, until now, the diagnosis of diseased tissue stay in CT, magnetic resonance (MR) and other imaging studies [2]. Conventional CT and MR can not found the cancer cells until they show abnormal morphological changes. Whereas the molecular imaging technology overcome the limitations of conventional imaging technology, which can detect the cancer cells in the early stage at the molecular level (i.e. before showing abnormal morphological changes) and is expected to enhance our ability to fight with cancer greatly. Furthermore, the MR/fluorescent bimodal molecular imaging technology provides both high-resolution histological structure information and high-sensitivity functional imaging and becomes a hot topic in recent years [3]. We are optimistic that the MR/fluorescent bimodal probe will make a great contribution in improving the care of cancer patients.

Comparing with semiconductor quantum dots which have been employed mostly to constructe MR/fluorescent bimodal probes combining with chelated gadolinium (3+) ions (Gd3+), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are just coming to play the same role [4]. Semiconductor quantum dots containing cadmium or other heavy metals are difficult to go through the kidneys and their toxicity prevents the further clinical application [5]. CQDs always prepared through simple procedure express excellent photoluminescence properties similar to that of the semiconductor quantum dots, which have attracted more and more interesting in heavy metals ions detection [6–8], Photocatalys [9], photoelectric conversion [10]. Besides, owing to the biocompatibility, CQDs have big potential to instead of semiconductor quantum dots especially for clinical use [11,12].

To date, there are only a few bimodal molecular imaging probes composed of Gd3+ and CQDs, furthermore, performances of the existed bimodal molecular imaging probes need to be improved urgently. Bourlinos et al. separately used tris (hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane as a carbon source and gadopentetic acid as a Gd3+ source to synthesize Gd3+-doped CQDs for MR/fluorescence bimodal imaging [13]. But neither the quantitative properties nor the doping form of the resultant Gd3+-doped CQDs were reported. In addition, the carbon source and Gd3+ source are provided by two kinds of materials, it is difficult to ensure the integrity of the Gd3+ and CQDs in Gd3+-doped CQDs. In our early study, gadopentetate monomeglumine (GdPM) was employed firstly as a source to provide carbon source and Gd3+ source simultaneously, and CQDs doped with Gd3+ chelates in molecular level were obtained [14]. However, both the photoluminescence and relaxation properties of the prepared CQDs were ordinary and hardly improved simultaneously. Moreover, Gong N., et al., prepared Gd3+-doped CQDs using sucrose solution, concentrated H2SO4, GdCl3 and diethylene glycol as precursors which must undergo complex pretreatment procedures before pyrolysis [15].

In this paper, Gd3+-codoped CQDs (Gd3+-CQDs) showing good photoluminescence as well as particularly excellent relaxation properties were prepared by hydrothermal method and employing gadopentetate monomeglumine as a precusor. The usual contradiction between the fluorescence intensity and the longitudinal relaxivity was solved by adjusting the hydrothermal conditions.

As a precursor, gadopentetate monomeglumine (GdPM, Shandong Hongfuda Pharmchem Co., Ltd) was pyrolyzed in a micro hydrothermal synthesis reactor (SPK-100ML, Binhai Puxin Instrument Co., Ltd) to prepare the Gd3+-CQDs through a process as follows: GdPM (0.24 g) was completely dissolved in deionized water (60 mL) and a homogeneous solution was obtained, followed by measuring its pH value (pH1). Then this solution was transferred into the reactor and pyrolyzed at a certain temperature for a period of time. When the pyrolyzed solution was cooled to room temperature, its pH value was measured too (pH2), and a yellow dispersion containing Gd3+-CQDs was obtained.

The pure Gd3+-CQDs was obtained from the yellow dispersion through a series of post-processing. Firstly, free Gd3+ ions that maybe present in the dispersion due to the thermal decomposition of the GdPM were removed by precipitation with NaOH; then, the resultant suspension was centrifuged (9000 rpm, 5 min) to remove Gd(OH)3 and dark brown (or black) insolubles; finally, pure Gd3+-CQDs were obtained by concentrating the supernatant, followed by drying at 105 °C.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence spectrometers were employed to characterize the optical properties of Gd3+-CQDs. The content of Gd3+ was determined by an inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometer (ICP, USA Thermo Fisher iCAP6500). The chemical environment of Gd3+ was confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific Company) with all the binding energies calibrated using C1s as the reference energy (285eV). The structure of the Gd3+-CQDs was analyzed by an infrared spectrometer (IR, PE Spectrum One) and an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, PANalytical B.V. X'Pert PRO). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Libra 200FE, Carl Zeiss SMT Pte Ltd) was used to characterize the Gd3+-CQDs concerning the structure, size, and size distribution.

The excitation and emission spectra (λexcitation = 365 nm) of Gd3 +-CQDs were obtained with an LS-55 Fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerKinELmer Instruments). The QY was calculated according to eqn (1):
(1)
Q Y = Q
Y R
I I
R
A R
A
n 2
n R 2
where R is the reference compound, I is the integrated emission intensity calculated from the area under the emission peak on the same wavelength scale (365 nm), A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, n is the refractive index of the solvent (for present case n/nR = 1). The reference compound used in the experiment is quinine sulfate (QYR = 0.55, dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4) [16].

The T1-weighted image and the longitudinal relaxation rate (r1/mM−1S−1) of Gd3+-CQDs were measured and compared with clinical Magnevist with the same concentration of Gd3+ ions (0.01 mM). To calculate the r1 of the Gd3+-CQDs, T1 was measured with a 3.0 T MRI scanner (GE Medical Systems) using an inversion recovery sequence, applying three different TI (TI = 100, TI = 300, TI = 600, TR/TE 4000 ms/9.2 ms). The r1 was estimated based on eqn (2) [17]. And T1-weighted images were obtained with an SE sequence (TR/TE = 300 ms/20 ms, Matrix 256 × 256 pixel, FOV 24 cm × 24 cm, NEX 1, slice thickness of 5 mm, gap 1.5 mm).
(2)
1 /
T 1
obsd
=
1 /
T 1
dia
+
r 1
×
M
where (1/T1)obsd is the observed relaxation rate of water proton in the presence of paramagnetic species, (1/T1)dia is the relaxation rate in the absence of the paramagnetic species and [M] is the molar concentration of the paramagnetic species.

The cytotoxicity of the Gd3+-CQDs was assessed using HeLa cells and was performed through the CCK-8 assay (Signalway Antibody, SAB) [18]. HeLa cells were placed in the 96-well plates (0.5 × 104 cells per well), and then cultured with RPMI 1640 (Hyclone) in a humidified incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 °C. After an incubation period of 24 h, different concentrations (12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200 μg mL−1) of the Gd3+-CQDs were added to each well of the 96-well plates. Cells cultured in the medium without Gd3+-CQDs were taken as the control. After incubation for another 24 h, CCK-8 solution was added to each well and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. Note that the optical density (OD) of each well was measured with a microplate reader at 450 nm. The value for cell viability was given as cell viability (%) = (ODsample−ODblank)/(ODcontrol−ODblank) × 100% [18], where the subscript blank denotes the wells without HeLa cells.

Based on previous research of our team, either low temperature or short time is not enough to pyrolyze and carbonize the GdPM, there is not favorable for the luminescence ability of the prepared CQDs. However, too high pyrolysis temperature or too long time will promote the decomposition of Gd3+-chelates in GdPM, and the loss of Gd3+ ions will inevitably reduce the relaxation rate of the Gd3+-doped CQDs. GdPM is composed of meglumine and gadopentetic acid units. During the pyrolysis of GdPM, the former unit with poor thermal stability decomposes prior to the latter, and releases small molecules such as H2O, CO2. When the pyrolysis temperature is lower than 350 °C, the Gd3+-N-O cyclic chelate structure in gadopentetic acid unit is stable. Therefore, the preliminary hydrothermal temperature in this paper is 200, 220, 250 and 270 °C, and the reaction time is 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 h, respectively.

After hydrothermal treated, the colorless transparent GdPM aqueous solution become yellow, and its acidity is decreased. As shown in Table 1 , the pH value of GdPM aqueous solution (ca. pH1 = 2.4) increases, even up to around 6.7 (pH2) when the GdPM aqueous solution is hydrothermal treated for 8 h, due to the cleavage of the hydroxyl and pendant carboxyl groups separately belonging to the meglumine and gadopentetic acid unit. And the pH2 value of GdPM aqueous solution increases significantly with the rise of hydrothermal temperature. Accompanying with that, the color of the hydrothermal treated GdPM solution gradually deepened, and dark brown (or black) insolubles appear when the temperature higher than 220 °C (Fig. S1 a). These phenomenons show that higher temperature is advantageous to the pyrolysis and carbonization of the GdPM.

Besides higher temperature, longer hydrothermal reaction time also shows the positive effect on the carbonization degree of GdPM. Fixed reaction temperature is 220 °C, when the reaction time is prolonged from 2 h to 10 h, the color of GdPM aqueous solution gradually deepened (Fig. S1 b), and the pH2 value increases from 2.5 to 5.8. However, an extravagantly long reaction time has little significance for the carbonization of GdPM, for example, when the reaction time is extended from 8 h to 10 h, the pH2 value show limited increase from 5.6 to 5.8 (Table 1).

In certain range, the increase of the carbonization degree is conducive to the photoluminescence of Gd3+-CQDs. Hence, increasing the reaction temperature and prolonging the reaction time have the same effects in getting better photoluminescence of Gd3+-CQDs. At low temperature by heat-treating the GdPM (200 °C, 220 °C, 250 °C and 270 °C, respectively), it could be found that the quantum yield of Gd3+-CQDs increased gradually (220 °C, 7.1%) with the increase of the hydrothermal reaction temperature. However, continuingly increased hydrothermal reaction temperature has unobvious significance; further raising reaction temperature to 270 °C, the photoluminescence quantum yield was even decreased to 6.2%, which may be attributed to the increase in the size of Gd3+-CQDs or excessive amounts of sp2 hybrid carbon atoms in the carbon nuclei [19]. On the other hand, with the gradual prolonging of the hydrothermal reaction time, when the temperature was fixed at 220 °C, the quantum yield of Gd3+-CQDs increased from 0.9% to 7.1% (corresponding to 2 h–8 h). And yet, the carbonization degree of GdPM did not show remarkable changes with the prolonging of the reaction time, and the quantum yield of as-prepared Gd3+-CQDs was decreased slightly, when the reaction time was more than 8 h.

Excessively high hydrothermal reaction temperature and too long reaction time, not only contributes little to enhancing the quantum yield of Gd3+-CQDs but also leads to the cracking of the Gd3+ chelates which further causes the decrease of the Gd3+-dopping amounts in Gd3+-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 , Gd3+-doping amounts have shown the overall trend of decline, excepting a slight increase when prepared at 220 °C for 8 h. For instance, the Gd3+ ions content in the Gd3+-CQDs prepared at 220 °C for 8 h (about 16.7 wt%) is slightly higher than that at 200 °C for 8 h and 220 °C for 6 h (both 16.2 wt%), probably due to the different cracking degrees of the meglumine and gadopentetic acid units in GdPM. It has been reported that the longitudinal relaxivity r
1 largely depends on direct interactions between Gd3+ ions and hydrogen protons, suggesting that outer surface-chelated Gd3+ of Gd3+-CQDs could contribute to r
1
[15].

Therefore, considering the quantum yield and Gd3+-dopping amounts, an optimal CQDs was defined in this paper as that obtained when the GdPM aqueous solution with a concentration of 4 mg mL−1 was pyrolyzed under 220 °C and 2.0 MPa for 8 h. Unless otherwise specified, the “Gd3+-CQDs” appears in subsequent text means this optimal CQDs.

Carbonaceous cores are generated from the carbonization of meglumine unit in GdPM after the GdPM is hydrothermal pyrolyzed, which are characteristic of amorphous mainly. As shown in the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images in Fig. 2 a and b, there is a well-developed cluster-like structure: each cluster is composed of many primary nanoparticles with a mean size of ∼1.6 nm (Fig. 2c, d). Unlike most of the previous reports, as-prepared carbon quantum dots prepared using glucose or citric acid as a precursor show well-resolved lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.21, 0.26, and 0.32 nm which are close to the (100), (020) and (002) diffraction facets of graphite carbon, respectively [18,20]. Here, the HRTEM image (inset in Fig. 2c and Fig. S3) of Gd3+-CQDs reveals one and only one lattice spacing of 0.31 nm, which agrees well with not only the (002) spacing of graphitic carbon but the (222) spacing of Gd2O3 (PDF 43-1014) as well [21]. Meanwhile, the electron diffraction pattern of Gd3+-CQDs insetted in Fig. 2c shows a typical amorphous character. Furthermore, rather than a sharp diffraction peak at 2θ = 26.4° attributing to the (002) plane (d = 0.34 nm) of graphite, a diffraction peakat ca. 28.0° belonging to the (222) crystal plane of Gd2O3 appears in the XRD curve of Gd3+-CQDs (Fig. S2 a) [21,22]. Hence, it is primarily suggested that the carbonaceous cores in Gd3+-CQDs grow without graphitization (agreeing well with our previous report in Ref. [14]), and are Gd2O3-doped amorphous carbon mainly.

In Gd3+-CQDs, the amorphous carbonaceous cores are doped by Gd3+ chelates and a small amount of Gd2O3. The XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 3 a clearly demonstrates the presence of Gd3+ in the Gd3+-CQDs. As expected, the high-resolution XPS (Fig. 3b, c) of Gd3+ in Gd3+-CQDs (obtained at a temperature lower than 220 °C for 8 h) exhibits peaks correlated to the electron levels of Gd3d and Gd4d positioned at 1186.0 eV and 141.9 eV, respectively, whose binding energy are obviously lower than those of free Gd3+ ions [23]. These results show that the Gd3+ ions in Gd3+-CQDs play the role of the central atom, whose binding energies slightly reduce when capture electrons from the ligands. However, the binding energy of Gd3d and Gd4d are increased when Gd3+-CQDs are prepared at higher temperature, especially increased to 1187.7 ev and 143.0 ev at 270 °C, respectively, which agree with the binding energy of Gd3+ in Gd2O3 [21,23]. This results consistent with that of XRD and HRTEM show that, in Gd3+-CODs, a few of Gd2O3 besides Gd3+ chelates are doped on the CQDs surface; and the amount of the Gd2O3 increases with the temperature as more obvious Gd2O3 peaks appear in the high-resolution XPS spectra.

There are several hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxylate (-COO-) on the surface of carbonaceous cores, although the Gd3+-CQDs are extracted from carbonized GdPM enduring harsh hydrothermal reaction. The IR spectrum of Gd3+-CQDs shows the same adsorption peaks as that of GdPM. The absorption bands centered at 1328 cm−1 is derived from the stretching mode of –C–N, the band at 1094 cm−1 is resulted from the stretching mode of the -OH, and a pair of bands at 1603 cm−1 and 1407 cm−1 are assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching modes of the –COO−, respectively (Fig. 3d). The hydroxyl groups are derived from partly carbonized meglumine unit of GdPM, and the -COO- groups are introduced by the gadopetic acid unit. It is -OH and -COO- groups endow Gd3+-CQDs good hydrophilic ability and support coordination site to Gd3+.

Gd3+-CQDs exhibit photoluminescence properties notably who emit bright blue fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm UV light, correlating to emission peak centered at 445 nm in the PL emission spectrum (Fig. 4 a). The UV–Vis spectrum of Gd3+-CQDs shows a characteristic absorption band of the CQDs at about 320 nm attributing to the n−π* transition (Fig. 4a) [24,25]. The Gd3+-CQDs (prepared at 220 °C for 8 h) possess the biggest fluorescence emission ability as demonstrated by its brightest blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. S1) and its biggest quantum yields (7.1%) than that prepared under other hydrothermal conditions. In addition, Gd3+-CQDs show classical PL properties of excitation-dependent emission, whose photoluminescence spectra redshift with about 20 nm increments of excitation wavelength (Fig. 4b) [16].

Here, as demonstrated above the Gd3+-CQDs show similar carbonaceous cores structure, and further semblable PL spectrum with our team reported Gd3+-dopped CQDs (Gd(III)/CQDs). It has been reported in Ref. [14] that, Gd(III)/CQDs with a diameter of ca. 2.4 nm and a quantum yield of ca. 3.4% can penetrate into cells showing bright fluorescence image. Therefore, the cell imaging experiment of Gd3+-CQDs was not performed in this paper, and we think that the smaller diameter (d = 1.6 nm) and higher quantum yield (7.1%) naturally lead to cell imaging applicability of Gd3+-CQDs.

The Gd3+-CQDs exhibit excellent MRI contrast strength, whose longitudinal relaxivity (r
1 ) is higher than the clinical Magnevist MRI contrast. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the Gd3+-CQDs show brighter positive signal than Magnevist and GdPM. The r
1 relaxivity of Gd3+-CQDs is 9.87 mM−1 s−1, higher than that prepared by our team previously.

To account for the enhancements of T
1 -weighted MRI contrast in our design, consider the following facts: (1) in the case of a nanoparticle-based MRI contrast agent, small size is important to reach a high r
1 because r
1 is largely dependent upon direct interactions between Gd3+ and hydrogen protons. The ultra-small Gd3+-CQDs with high specific surface area could maximize dipole–dipole interactions between Gd3+ ions and hydrogen protons. Meanwhile, Gd3+-CQDs has excellent hydrophilicity, thus, are responsible for the enhanced r
1
[15,26,27]. (2) In the Gd3+-CQDs, Gd3+ ions are loaded on ultra-small carbonaceous cores (smaller than 1.6 nm), forming considerable quantities of confined Gd3+ clusters which greatly enhance the relaxation properties by way of magnetic proximity effects [28]. (3) The Gd3+-CQDs can hug each other and form so-called colloidal nanoparticles as secondary clusters (in Fig. 1a and b). The secondary clusters not only enhance the relaxation properties by way of magnetic proximity effects as mention above, but also take advantage of the interactions between neighboring nanoparticles resulting in new properties (e.g. very high r
1 ) not present in the original constituents [29]. (4) Since the carbonization of GdPM is carried out in the air atmosphere the as-prepared Gd3+-CQDs contain a small amount of Gd2O3 besides gadolinium chelates.

It is reasonable that the Gd3+-CQDs do not show any serious cytotoxicity because the Gd3+ is stably chelated by biocompatible CQDs whose surfaces were decorated with abundant hydroxyl/carboxyl hydrophilic groups. Here, CCK-8 tests were conducted on human HeLa cells whose viability was tested after being exposed to the Gd3+-CQDs at different concentrations for 24 h. The HeLa cells incubated with low concentration of Gd3+-CQDs show comparative viability, as shown in Fig. 6 , which is as high as 98% in Gd3+-CQDs with a concentration of 12.5 mg mL−1. And the viability is not obviously decreased until the concentration of Gd3+-CQDs is more than 50 μg mL−1. And even about 77% of HeLa cells remain alive in the presence of Gd3+-CQDs with a concentration as high as 200 μg mL−1. Consequently, thanks to excellent photoluminescence properties, incomparable relaxation properties and low toxicity, Gd3+-CQDs has an enormous potential to be used as a MR/fluorescence bimodal probe.

In this paper, Gd3+-CQDs were prepared employing GdPM as a precursor and by hydrothermal method which did prepare CQDs with high longitudinal relaxation rate and high quantum yield (7.1%) at low temperature facilely. The surface of Gd3+-CQDs was shown to contain the Gd3+ chelates, Gd2O3, and hydrophilic functional groups (-OH, -COO-) stabilizing the particles in water solution. The size of Gd3+-CQDs, as determined by TEM, was approximately 1.6 nm. The CCK-8 assay disclosed that the Gd3+-CQDs had a minor impact on the HeLa cells viability, since Gd3+ ions were mainly chelated with the biocompatible CQDs. Moreover, the Gd3+-CQDs led to an enhanced MRI contrast with a r
1 value 9.87 mM−1 s−1. In comparison to Magnevist, Gd3+-CQDs have more advantages on magnetic resonance imaging, which can greatly reduce the dosage for “anchoring” clinical lesions. These features make Gd3+-CQDs very promising candidates for biomedical applications as MR/fluorescence bimodal probe.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 81171318) and Shaanxi Health Department Foundation, China (no. 2010E07).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2016.04.018.
